categories.description: State and central government welfare schemes available in Andhra Pradesh

contentItems.description: Child Helpline - For children in distress

contentItems.title: Right to Equality
contentItems.description: Article 14-18 - Equal treatment before law regardless of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth

contentItems.title: Right to Freedom
contentItems.description: Article 19-22 - Freedom of speech, assembly, association, movement and profession

contentItems.title: Right against Exploitation
contentItems.description: Article 23-24 - Prohibition of human trafficking and forced labour

contentItems.title: Right to Freedom of Religion
contentItems.description: Articles 25–28  - Freedom to practice, profess, and propagate religion

contentItems.title: Cultural and Educational Rights
contentItems.description: Articles 29–30 -Protects rights of minorities to preserve language, culture, and establish educational institutions

contentItems.title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
contentItems.description: Article 32 - Citizens can approach courts directly if Fundamental Rights are violated. Dr. B. R. Ambedkar called this the “heart and soul” of the Constitution

contentItems.title: Right to Equality
contentItems.description: Equality before the law and equal protection of the laws within India.

contentItems.title: Prohibition of Discrimination
contentItems.description: Prohibits discrimination by the State on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth.

contentItems.title: Equality of Opportunity in Public Employment
contentItems.description: Guarantees equality of opportunity in matters of public employment and government jobs.

contentItems.title: Abolition of Untouchability
contentItems.description: Abolishes "untouchability" and makes its practice punishable by law.

contentItems.title: Freedom of Speech and Expression
contentItems.description: Guarantees freedom of speech and expression, subject to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2).

contentItems.title: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
contentItems.description: No person shall be deprived of life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.

contentItems.title: Right to Education
contentItems.description: Provides free and compulsory education to all children aged 6–14 years.

contentItems.title: Protection Against Arrest and Detention
contentItems.description: Provides safeguards against arbitrary arrest and detention, including the right to be informed of grounds of arrest and consult a lawyer.

contentItems.title: Freedom of Religion
contentItems.description: Freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion, subject to public order, morality, and health.

contentItems.title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
contentItems.description: Empowers individuals to approach the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.
Sexual intercourse by husband during separation
Sexual intercourse by deceitful means, false promise of marriage, etc.
Assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage her modesty
Word, gesture or act intended to insult modesty of a woman
Cruelty by husband or relatives of husband
Culpable homicide not amounting to murder
Voluntarily causing grievous hurt or hurt by dangerous weapons/means
Wrongful confinement for three or more days
Waging war against Government of India
Acts endangering sovereignty, unity and integrity of India
Deliberate acts intended to outrage religious feelings
Theft in dwelling house/place of worship/transport
Robbery or dacoity with attempt to cause death or grievous hurt
Forgery of valuable security, will, etc.
Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace
Breach of contract to attend on helpless person

contentItems.title: Right to Information Act, 2005 (RTI Act)
contentItems.description: Enables citizens to obtain information from public authorities and promotes transparency and accountability in government.

contentItems.title: Consumer Protection Act, 2019
contentItems.description: Protects consumers against unfair trade practices, defective products, and deficient services.

contentItems.title: Information Technology Act, 2000 (IT Act)
contentItems.description: Governs electronic records, digital transactions, cybersecurity, and cyber offences.

contentItems.title: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (POCSO Act)
contentItems.description: Protects children from sexual assault, sexual harassment, and pornography-related offences.

contentItems.title: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
contentItems.description: Provides civil remedies and protection for women facing domestic violence.

contentItems.title: Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (as amended)
contentItems.description: Regulates motor vehicles, driving licences, road safety, traffic rules, and penalties.

contentItems.title: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
contentItems.description: Governs matters relating to children in conflict with law and children requiring care and protection.

contentItems.title: Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989
contentItems.description: Prevents atrocities, discrimination, and violence against Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.

contentItems.title: Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987
contentItems.description: Provides free legal aid and ensures access to justice for eligible persons.

contentItems.title: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013
contentItems.description: Protects women from sexual harassment at workplaces and establishes complaint mechanisms.

contentItems.title: Persons with Disabilities (Rights of Persons with Disabilities) Act, 2016
contentItems.description: Protects the rights and dignity of persons with disabilities and promotes equal opportunities.

contentItems.title: Mental Healthcare Act, 2017
contentItems.description: Protects rights of persons with mental illness and guarantees access to mental healthcare services.

contentItems.title: Whistle Blowers Protection Act, 2014
contentItems.description: Protects individuals who report corruption, misuse of power, or public wrongdoing.

contentItems.title: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS)
contentItems.description: Defines criminal offences and punishments in India, replacing the Indian Penal Code, 1860.

contentItems.title: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS)
contentItems.description: Governs criminal procedure including arrest, investigation, bail, and trial, replacing the CrPC, 1973.

contentItems.title: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (BSA)
contentItems.description: Governs admissibility and evaluation of evidence in courts, replacing the Indian Evidence Act, 1872.

contentItems.title: FIR Registration
contentItems.description: Police must register an FIR when information discloses a cognizable offence.

contentItems.title: Right to Lawyer
contentItems.description: A person arrested or accused has the right to consult and be defended by an advocate of their choice.

contentItems.title: Right to be Informed of Grounds of Arrest
contentItems.description: Police must clearly inform the arrested person of the reasons for arrest.

contentItems.title: Right to Inform Relative/Friend
contentItems.description: Police must inform a relative, friend, or nominated person about the arrest and place of detention.

contentItems.title: Arrest Memo
contentItems.description: Police must prepare an arrest memo containing the date, time, place of arrest, and witness details.

contentItems.title: Medical Examination
contentItems.description: An arrested person has the right to medical examination under prescribed procedures.

contentItems.title: Free Legal Aid
contentItems.description: Eligible persons who cannot afford legal representation are entitled to free legal aid.

contentItems.title: Production Before Magistrate
contentItems.description: An arrested person must generally be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time).

contentItems.title: Protection Against Illegal Detention
contentItems.description: No person may be detained beyond the legally permitted period without judicial authorization.

contentItems.title: Right to Bail
contentItems.description: In bailable offences, the accused has a right to be released on bail subject to legal requirements.

contentItems.title: Right Against Self-Incrimination
contentItems.description: No person can be compelled to be a witness against themselves.

contentItems.title: Right to Silence
contentItems.description: An accused person has the right to remain silent and is not required to confess guilt.

contentItems.title: Right to Copy of FIR
contentItems.description: Victims/complainants are entitled to receive a copy of the FIR free of cost.

contentItems.title: Right to Zero FIR
contentItems.description: An FIR may be registered at any police station regardless of territorial jurisdiction and later transferred to the appropriate station.

contentItems.title: Rights of Women During Arrest
contentItems.description: Special safeguards apply to the arrest, search, and examination of women under the law.

contentItems.title: NTR Bharosa Pension Scheme – Social security pension scheme for vulnerable citizens
contentItems.description: Senior citizens, widows, disabled persons, transgender persons, and other eligible categories

hierarchyPositions.title: The Speaker
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The Speaker is like the referee of the Assembly. They sit on the high chair at the top, keep order during debates, decide who may speak, and make sure laws are passed fairly. Students should know: the Speaker does not vote on party lines — they serve all MLAs equally.

hierarchyPositions.title: Assembly Secretary (Left)
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Officials beside the Speaker help record every word spoken, prepare bills and reports, and guide MLAs on Assembly rules. They support democracy by keeping accurate records of what government decides.

hierarchyPositions.title: Assembly Secretary (Right)
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: These officers assist the Speaker with documents, timing of votes, and communication between the Assembly and the State government departments.

hierarchyPositions.title: The Well (Centre Table)
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The large centre table in front of the Speaker is called "the well." Ministers and senior officials sit here when important bills are introduced. This is where major policy discussions happen before voting on the green and red benches.

hierarchyPositions.title: Chief Minister
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The Chief Minister leads the State government. They sit on the Treasury side (green chairs, left in this diagram) in the front row nearest the centre aisle. The CM answers questions, presents the budget, and guides ministers to implement schemes for Andhra Pradesh.

hierarchyPositions.title: Deputy Chief Minister
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The Deputy CM supports the Chief Minister and may head key departments. Sitting beside the CM on green benches shows they are part of the ruling government team responsible for day-to-day governance.

hierarchyPositions.title: Cabinet Minister
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Cabinet Ministers head departments such as Education, Health, or IT. Example: Nara Lokesh oversees technology and governance reforms. Ministers on green benches propose laws and defend government policies in debates.

hierarchyPositions.title: Ruling Party MLAs
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Members of Legislative Assembly (MLAs) on the Treasury side vote with the government. They represent constituencies across Andhra Pradesh, raise local issues, and pass the state budget and laws. Green benches = ruling party side.

hierarchyPositions.title: Leader of Opposition
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The Leader of Opposition leads the largest party not in power. They sit on the red benches (right side) opposite the CM. Their job is to question government decisions, suggest alternatives, and represent voters who did not support the ruling party.

hierarchyPositions.title: Deputy Leader of Opposition
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: The Deputy LoP assists the Opposition Leader during debates and committee work. They coordinate YSR Congress MLAs and ensure the opposition's voice is heard on budgets, welfare schemes, and local issues.

hierarchyPositions.title: Senior Opposition MLA
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Senior opposition MLAs have experience in Assembly rules and policy. They speak on important bills and hold the government accountable — a key part of democracy students learn in civics class.

hierarchyPositions.title: Opposition MLAs
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Opposition MLAs sit on red benches in curved rows. They debate, vote, and raise constituency problems even when their party is not in power. Red benches = opposition side; this two-sided layout helps students see balance in democracy.

hierarchyPositions.title: Press Gallery
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Reporters sit in the gallery to watch Assembly sessions and inform the public. A free press helps citizens understand what laws are made and how leaders debate — an important link between the Assembly and everyday people.

hierarchyPositions.title: Public Gallery
hierarchyPositions.responsibilities: Citizens, including students on educational visits, can watch Assembly proceedings from the public gallery. Seeing democracy in action helps you understand how leaders are accountable to the people of Andhra Pradesh.

quizQuestions.question: Who is the constitutional head of Andhra Pradesh?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Governor is the constitutional head of the state.

quizQuestions.question: How many MLAs are in the AP Legislative Assembly?
quizQuestions.explanation: Andhra Pradesh has 175 Assembly constituencies.

quizQuestions.question: Which helpline is for medical emergencies?
quizQuestions.explanation: 102 is the ambulance/medical emergency helpline.

quizQuestions.question: Which article range covers the Right to Equality?
quizQuestions.explanation: Right to Equality is covered under Articles 14 to 18.

quizQuestions.question: Which right includes freedom of speech and expression?
quizQuestions.explanation: Right to Freedom includes freedoms in Article 19.

quizQuestions.question: Prohibition of human trafficking is part of which right?
quizQuestions.explanation: Articles 23–24 prohibit trafficking and forced labour.

quizQuestions.question: Freedom of religion is covered under which articles?
quizQuestions.explanation: Right to Freedom of Religion is Articles 25 to 28.

quizQuestions.question: Cultural and educational rights are under which articles?
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultural and Educational Rights are Articles 29 and 30.

quizQuestions.question: Who is the head of the state government in Andhra Pradesh?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Chief Minister heads the elected state government.

quizQuestions.question: Who presides over sessions of the Andhra Pradesh Legislative Assembly?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Speaker conducts Assembly business and maintains order.

quizQuestions.question: How many constituencies are there in the Andhra Pradesh Legislative Assembly?
quizQuestions.explanation: Andhra Pradesh has 175 Assembly constituencies.

quizQuestions.question: Which house represents states in the Parliament of India?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is the Council of States.

quizQuestions.question: Members of Lok Sabha are elected by
quizQuestions.explanation: Lok Sabha members are directly elected by voters.

quizQuestions.question: Who is the administrative head of a district?
quizQuestions.explanation: The District Collector leads civil administration.

quizQuestions.question: Who is responsible for law and order in a district police administration?
quizQuestions.explanation: SP heads the district police (law and order).

quizQuestions.question: Mandals are managed by which officer?
quizQuestions.explanation: MRO is the key administrative officer at mandal level.

quizQuestions.question: Which helpline number is for police emergency in India?
quizQuestions.explanation: 100 is for Police emergencies (legacy).

quizQuestions.question: Which helpline number is for fire emergencies?
quizQuestions.explanation: 101 is the Fire emergency number (legacy).

quizQuestions.question: 1098 is known as
quizQuestions.explanation: 1098 is the Child Helpline.

quizQuestions.question: The Constitution of India is the
quizQuestions.explanation: It is the supreme law of India.

quizQuestions.question: A fundamental duty is
quizQuestions.explanation: Fundamental Duties describe responsibilities of citizens.

quizQuestions.question: The judiciary’s main job is to
quizQuestions.explanation: Courts interpret laws and ensure justice.

quizQuestions.question: Andhra Pradesh is a state in
quizQuestions.explanation: Andhra Pradesh is in South India.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a famous temple town in Andhra Pradesh?
quizQuestions.explanation: Tirupati is widely known for Sri Venkateswara Temple.

quizQuestions.question: A bill becomes a law after it is
options: Passed by the legislature and gets assent
Signed by a district collector only
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Passed by the legislature and gets assent
quizQuestions.explanation: Bills require passage and assent as per procedure.

quizQuestions.question: Elections help citizens to
quizQuestions.explanation: Elections are for choosing representatives.

quizQuestions.question: The right to vote in India is given to citizens who are
quizQuestions.explanation: Voting age is 18.

courtPositions.title: Chief Justice / Judge
courtPositions.designation: Judge's Bench — High Court of AP
courtPositions.responsibilities: The Chief Justice (or presiding judge) sits on the elevated bench at the top. They hear cases, interpret law, deliver judgments, and maintain discipline in court. Students should know: the judge is impartial and does not take sides.

courtPositions.title: Judges' Entry
courtPositions.responsibilities: Judges enter the courtroom through this door before hearings begin. It keeps the court process formal and separates judicial officers from the public gallery.

courtPositions.title: Court Officers' Entry
courtPositions.responsibilities: Court masters, stenographers, and administrative staff use this entry. They support the judge by managing case files, calling matters, and recording proceedings.

courtPositions.title: Senior Advocates
courtPositions.responsibilities: Senior advocates sit at the central table facing the judge. They present legal arguments, cite precedents, and examine witnesses on behalf of their clients in important cases.

courtPositions.title: Accused
courtPositions.responsibilities: A person accused in a criminal case or respondent in certain matters stands or sits here. They have the right to a fair trial and a lawyer to defend them. The court protects their legal rights.

courtPositions.title: Victim
courtPositions.responsibilities: Victims or complainants may be seated here in criminal cases. The court ensures their safety and dignity while the State prosecutes the accused. Victim rights are an important part of justice.

courtPositions.title: Court Officers (Left)
courtPositions.responsibilities: Court officers on the left record proceedings, manage exhibits, and assist the judge. Accurate records help appeals and ensure transparency in how justice is delivered.

courtPositions.title: Court Officers (Right)
courtPositions.responsibilities: Staff on the right wing support filing of petitions, scheduling of cases, and communication between the bench and advocates. The registry keeps the High Court functioning smoothly.

courtPositions.title: Government Advocates
courtPositions.responsibilities: Government advocates represent the State of Andhra Pradesh. They defend government actions, prosecute in criminal appeals, and advise departments on legal matters before the High Court.

courtPositions.title: Defense Advocates
courtPositions.responsibilities: Private advocates defend citizens, companies, or the accused. Everyone has the right to legal representation. These desks are where lawyers prepare arguments and question witnesses.

courtPositions.title: Registrar
courtPositions.responsibilities: The Registrar administers the High Court under the Chief Justice — managing case flow, judicial roster, and administrative rules. Students can think of this role as the chief administrator of the court system.

courtPositions.title: Public Gallery
courtPositions.responsibilities: The public gallery is where citizens, journalists, and students may watch court proceedings. Open courts teach us that justice should be transparent and accountable to the people of Andhra Pradesh.

courtAuthorities.name: Smt. Justice Lisa Gill
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Head of the AP High Court at Amaravati. Sworn in 25 April 2026. Assigns benches and leads judicial administration. Source: aphc.gov.in.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice Cheekati Manavendranath Roy
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Former Registrar General of AP High Court; hears civil, criminal, and constitutional matters as per roster.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice Ravi Nath Tilhari
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Hears writ petitions, appeals, and service matters assigned by the Chief Justice.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice Rao Raghunandan Rao
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Former Senior Advocate; handles commercial, constitutional, and regulatory matters.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice Battu Devanand
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Former Government Pleader; adjudicates matters across civil and criminal jurisdictions.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice Donadi Ramesh
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Former Special Government Pleader; hears service and revenue disputes.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice B. Krishna Mohan
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Participates in division and single-judge benches as per court roster.

courtAuthorities.name: Sri Justice K. Suresh Reddy
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Elevated 2020; practices in civil, criminal, and constitutional matters.

courtAuthorities.name: Registrar General
courtAuthorities.designation: Registrar General, High Court of AP
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Chief administrative officer — case listing, registry, and court establishment. Source: aphc.gov.in.

courtAuthorities.name: Advocate General of Andhra Pradesh
courtAuthorities.responsibilities: Represents the State of Andhra Pradesh before the High Court through the Advocate General and government pleaders.

districts.name: NTR
districts.spName: Verify on NTR district police portal

policeAuthorities.name: Harish Kumar Gupta, IPS
policeAuthorities.details: Head of Andhra Pradesh Police (appointed 2025). Overall law & order, policy, and coordination of state police forces. Source: State government notifications / press.

policeAuthorities.name: Smt. Anitha Vangalapudi
policeAuthorities.details: Cabinet minister for Home Affairs & Disaster Management — political head overseeing police administration at state level.

policeAuthorities.name: As per ap police hierarchy
policeAuthorities.details: Assists DGP on law and order, crime, and internal security across zones.

policeAuthorities.name: Zone / wing heads
policeAuthorities.details: Supervises police ranges, CID, traffic, railways, or other specialised wings.

policeAuthorities.name: Range officers
policeAuthorities.details: Oversees a group of districts or a commissionerate within a police zone.

policeAuthorities.name: District level
policeAuthorities.details: Heads district police (except police commissionerates). Each revenue district has an SP — see Districts tab for district-wise names on ap.gov.in.

parliamentTopics.title: What is Lok Sabha?
parliamentTopics.summary: The House of the People — lower house of Parliament.
parliamentTopics.content:
Lok Sabha (House of the People) is the lower house of India's Parliament. Members are called MPs (Members of Parliament).

• Maximum strength: 552 members (530 from states, 20 from UTs, up to 2 Anglo-Indian nominees).
• Andhra Pradesh sends 25 MPs to Lok Sabha.
• Lok Sabha makes laws on national subjects like defence, railways, currency, and foreign policy.
• The party or alliance with a majority in Lok Sabha usually forms the Union Government at the Centre.

parliamentTopics.title: How are Lok Sabha members elected?
parliamentTopics.summary: Direct election by voters in each parliamentary constituency.
parliamentTopics.content:
Lok Sabha MPs are elected through direct elections:

1. India is divided into parliamentary constituencies (Andhra Pradesh has 25 Lok Sabha seats).
2. Voters cast ONE vote for a candidate in their constituency.
3. The candidate with the highest votes (First Past the Post system) wins that seat.
4. Elections are held every 5 years, or earlier if the house is dissolved.
5. Universal adult franchise: citizens aged 18+ can vote.

Example: If Constituency X has 8 lakh voters, the candidate with the most votes becomes MP — even if they did not get 50% of all votes.

parliamentTopics.title: What is Rajya Sabha?
parliamentTopics.summary: The Council of States — upper house of Parliament.
parliamentTopics.content:
Rajya Sabha (Council of States) is the upper house of Parliament. Members are called RS MPs.

• Maximum strength: 250 (238 elected + 12 nominated by President for arts, science, literature).
• Andhra Pradesh has 11 Rajya Sabha seats.
• Rajya Sabha represents states; it cannot be dissolved.
• It reviews laws passed by Lok Sabha and protects state interests.
• One-third of Rajya Sabha members retire every 2 years (6-year term each).

parliamentTopics.title: How are Rajya Sabha members elected?
parliamentTopics.summary: Elected by MLAs of state legislatures — not by direct public vote.
parliamentTopics.content:
Rajya Sabha MPs are NOT elected directly by citizens:

1. Members are elected by the elected MLAs of State Legislative Assemblies (and UTs with legislatures).
2. Voting uses the Single Transferable Vote (STV) system with proportional representation.
3. Each state gets seats based on population — larger states send more members.
4. The President nominates 12 members with special knowledge in literature, science, art, and social service.

For students: When you vote in Assembly elections, your MLA later participates in choosing Rajya Sabha MPs from your state.

parliamentTopics.title: What is a ruling party? How many seats are needed?
parliamentTopics.summary: Majority in Lok Sabha (272+ out of 543 elected seats) to form government.
parliamentTopics.content:
Ruling Party / Government at Centre:

• After Lok Sabha elections, the President invites the leader who can prove a majority to become Prime Minister.
• Majority means more than half of the effective strength of Lok Sabha.
• Out of 543 elected seats, the majority mark is 272 seats (half of 543 is 271.5, so 272 is needed).
• The ruling party (or alliance) with 272+ seats runs the Union Government.

State level (Andhra Pradesh Assembly): 175 seats → majority mark is 88 seats to form the state government.

parliamentTopics.title: What if no party gets a majority?
parliamentTopics.summary: Hung Parliament — parties form alliances or coalitions.
parliamentTopics.content:
If no single party wins 272 Lok Sabha seats, it is called a Hung Parliament:

1. Parties negotiate to form a coalition government.
2. Smaller parties may support a larger party in return for ministries and policies.
3. A Confidence Vote in Lok Sabha proves the government has majority support.
4. If the government loses a no-confidence vote, it must resign and new elections may be held.

Alliance (Mahagathbandhan / NDA style):
• Pre-poll alliance: Parties agree before elections to share seats and campaign together.
• Post-poll alliance: Parties join after results to cross the 272-seat mark together.
• Example: NDA, UPA, or state-level alliances like TDP+JSP+BJP in AP.

ministerOsds.ministry: Chief Minister — General Administration, Law & Order
ministerOsds.officerType: Ex-officio Special Chief Secretary to CM
ministerOsds.osdDetails: Spl. CS to CM & Energy (Mar 2026). CMO secretariat: prlsecy-cm@ap.gov.in · 0863-2446637. Source: ap.gov.in / Sarkaritel (Dec 2025).

assemblyTopics.title: What is the State Assembly?
assemblyTopics.summary: Andhra Pradesh Legislative Assembly — the state legislature with 175 MLAs.
assemblyTopics.content:
The State Legislative Assembly (Vidhana Sabha) is the lower house of the state legislature. In Andhra Pradesh it has 175 elected Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs).

• MLAs are chosen by voters in Assembly constituencies (one MLA per constituency).
• The Assembly makes laws on state subjects — education, police, agriculture, health, local bodies, etc.
• It passes the state budget and holds the government accountable through questions and debates.
• The Assembly meets at the legislature complex (Amaravati / Velagapudi area) when the Governor summons sessions.

For students: When you hear "AP Assembly", it means these 175 elected representatives of the people of Andhra Pradesh.

assemblyTopics.title: How is the Assembly conducted?
assemblyTopics.summary: Sessions led by the Speaker; Treasury and Opposition benches; laws passed by vote.
assemblyTopics.content:
Assembly proceedings follow rules laid down in the Constitution and the Assembly's own rules:

1. The Governor opens or prorogues sessions; the Speaker presides over day-to-day business.
2. MLAs sit on Treasury (ruling) or Opposition benches — see the Assembly Hierarchy diagram in Learn Hierarchies.
3. Bills are introduced, debated in the House, and passed by a majority vote.
4. The Speaker decides who may speak, maintains order, and does not vote except to break a tie.
5. Officials (Secretariat) record proceedings and assist with legislative drafting.
6. Important sessions include budget session, monsoon session, and winter session.

Students can relate this to a school parliament: agenda, debate, vote, and minutes — but at state government level.

assemblyTopics.title: How is the CM chosen and government formed?
assemblyTopics.summary: The Governor invites the leader who can prove majority support among MLAs.
assemblyTopics.content:
After Assembly elections:

1. All 175 seats are filled by elected MLAs.
2. Political parties count their seats. The party or alliance with a majority (more than half) usually forms the government.
3. The Governor invites the leader of that majority group to become Chief Minister (CM).
4. The CM chooses ministers from MLAs (and rarely, non-MLAs where permitted) to run departments.
5. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Assembly — it must retain the confidence of the House.

In Andhra Pradesh (2024): The NDA alliance (TDP, Jana Sena, BJP) won a large majority; N. Chandrababu Naidu was sworn in as Chief Minister.

assemblyTopics.title: What is the ruling party? How many seats are needed?
assemblyTopics.summary: Majority in AP = more than half of 175 seats → at least 88 seats.
assemblyTopics.content:
Ruling party / ruling alliance:

• The group that has a majority of MLAs in the Assembly runs the state government.
• Andhra Pradesh Assembly strength: 175 seats.
• Majority mark: more than 175 ÷ 2 → at least 88 seats (87 is not enough; 88 is the minimum majority).

Examples:
• If one party wins 90 seats, it can form the government alone.
• If Party A wins 70 and Party B wins 25, together they have 95 — an alliance can form the government.

The ruling party occupies the Treasury benches in the Assembly; the largest opposition group sits on the Opposition benches.

assemblyTopics.title: What if no party gets a majority?
assemblyTopics.summary: Hung Assembly — parties form coalitions or alliances; confidence vote decides.
assemblyTopics.content:
If no single party wins 88 or more seats, it is called a Hung Assembly:

1. Parties negotiate post-poll alliances to cross the majority mark together.
2. The Governor may invite the largest party or alliance to try to form the government.
3. The proposed CM must prove majority support through a floor test (confidence vote) on the Assembly floor.
4. If the government loses a no-confidence motion, it must resign; the Governor may invite another combination or call fresh elections.

Pre-poll alliance: Parties agree before elections to share seats and campaign together (e.g. TDP + JSP + BJP in 2024).

Post-poll alliance: Parties join after results to reach 88+ seats together.

At the national level, Lok Sabha needs 272+ out of 543 elected seats — the same idea, different numbers.

grievanceTopics.title: Andhra Pradesh Grievance Redressal System (Spandana / PGRS) — Overview
grievanceTopics.summary: One system for complaints against departments, schemes, delays, certificates, pensions, roads, and more.
grievanceTopics.content:
The Government of Andhra Pradesh uses the Spandana / Public Grievance Redressal System (PGRS) for citizens to raise complaints against government departments, officers, schemes, delays, corruption, public issues, certificates, pensions, roads, electricity, police matters, revenue issues, and related matters.

This is a "one-spot" platform linking:

• GSWS (Grama / Ward Sachivalayam)
• 1902 call centre (24×7)
• Web portal (pgrs.ap.gov.in / meekosam.ap.gov.in)
• Mobile app
• Spandana Monday at Collectorates
• CM Office monitoring

RTI applications are generally not handled through Spandana — use the RTI process separately.

grievanceTopics.title: Official portals & contact details
grievanceTopics.content:
Main grievance portal — AP Spandana / PGRS:
• https://pgrs.ap.gov.in
• https://meekosam.ap.gov.in (CPGRAMS / state PGRS)

Government service information:
• AP government services portal — grievance registration section (search "AP grievance registration" on ap.gov.in)

Grievance status check:
• Track on pgrs.ap.gov.in or meekosam.ap.gov.in using your YSR# / grievance ID

Helpline numbers:
• Toll-free: 1902 (primary — Jaganannaku Chebudham / CM PGRS)
• Alternate toll-free: 1800-425-4440

Email:
• helpspandana-ap@ap.gov.in
• pgrs-helpdesk@ap.gov.in (PGRS helpdesk)
• jkchelpdesk-ap@ap.gov.in (CM helpdesk)

Office (CMGRS): Block 1, Secretariat, Velagapudi, Amaravati, Andhra Pradesh

grievanceTopics.title: What type of complaints can be raised?
grievanceTopics.summary: Revenue, police, municipal, welfare, pensions, roads, electricity, and more.
grievanceTopics.content:
You can file grievances related to:

• Revenue Department (land, pattadar passbook, certificates)
• Police Department (law & order, harassment — not substitute for FIR in serious crimes)
• Municipal Administration (tax, drains, town planning)
• Panchayat Raj (village roads, rural issues)
• Roads & Buildings (R&B)
• Electricity (DISCOM bills, connections, outages)
• Water Supply
• Welfare Schemes & pension delays
• Ration card problems
• Land disputes & encroachments
• Corruption complaints against government staff
• Delay in certificates (income, caste, residence, etc.)
• Education (schools, scholarships)
• Health (hospitals, Aarogyasri / NTR Vaidya Seva)
• Transport & APSRTC
• Housing & TIDCO
• Agriculture & horticulture schemes
• Village / Ward Secretariat service denial
• Government employee misconduct

Choose the correct department when filing so the complaint routes to the right officer.

grievanceTopics.title: Departments connected to PGRS (30+ major departments)
grievanceTopics.summary: Complaints auto-route to Revenue, Police, Municipal, Welfare, Education, Health, and others.
grievanceTopics.content:
The grievance system is integrated with almost all Andhra Pradesh government departments. Major departments connected include:

• Revenue Department
• Home / Police Department
• Municipal Administration & Urban Development
• Panchayat Raj & Rural Development
• Energy Department
• Water Resources / Irrigation
• Education & Higher Education
• Health & Family Welfare
• Women & Child Welfare
• Social Welfare, BC Welfare, SC Welfare, ST Welfare, Minority Welfare
• Housing Department
• Agriculture, Horticulture, Fisheries
• Transport & APSRTC
• Civil Supplies (ration)
• Labour Department
• Registration & Stamps, Endowments
• Forest Department
• Industries & IT
• Collectorates & Village/Ward Secretariat System
• CM Office Monitoring Cell

There are usually 30+ major departments and hundreds of subordinate offices. Complaints are routed automatically to the concerned authority at district or mandal level.

grievanceTopics.title: Method 1 — Online portal: complete step-by-step (Steps 1–8)
grievanceTopics.summary: Best method — register on portal, fill details, select department, upload proof, get YSR# number.
grievanceTopics.content:
Step 1 — Open portal
Visit https://pgrs.ap.gov.in or https://meekosam.ap.gov.in

Step 2 — Login / authentication
Usually through:
• Mobile OTP
• Aadhaar-based authentication
• Citizen credentials (if already registered)

Step 3 — Click "Register Grievance"
Choose options such as: New Complaint / Public Grievance / Lodge Complaint

Step 4 — Fill citizen details
Enter:
• Name
• Mobile number
• Aadhaar (optional or required depending on module)
• Address, District, Mandal, Village/Ward

Step 5 — Select department
Choose the department related to your issue.
Examples:
• Land issue → Revenue
• Police harassment → Home Department
• Pension delay → Welfare Department
• Road issue → Panchayat Raj / R&B

Step 6 — Write complaint clearly
Mention: problem, date, officer name (if any), location, supporting facts.
Best format:
• What happened
• Since when
• Which office/officer involved
• What action you want

Step 7 — Upload supporting documents (optional)
Aadhaar copy, photos, certificates, applications, receipts, screenshots.

Step 8 — Submit complaint
After submission a unique complaint number is generated — often called YSR# (Your Spandana Request Number). Save this carefully for all follow-up and tracking.

grievanceTopics.title: What happens after submission (internal workflow)
grievanceTopics.summary: Registration → routing → GRA assignment → field enquiry → action → closure.
grievanceTopics.content:
1. Complaint registration
System generates YSR#, timestamp, and department mapping.

2. Routing to department
Complaint goes automatically to district office, mandal office, or concerned department officer.

3. Assignment to GRA (Grievance Redressal Authority)
GRA officer reviews complaint, sends to subordinate, requests field verification.

4. Field enquiry
Officials may visit location, call complainant, verify documents, seek reports from local officers.

5. Action taken
Possible actions: service approval, file movement, official warning, order issuance, resolution update, or rejection with remarks.

6. Closure
Complaint marked: Resolved / Closed / Rejected / Action Taken.
Citizen usually receives SMS update and portal status update.

If not satisfied — reopen grievance online, call 1902, visit Secretariat/Collectorate, or file escalation complaint (reopened cases go to higher authorities).

grievanceTopics.title: Other ways to file complaints (Call, Secretariat, Spandana Monday, App)
grievanceTopics.summary: 1902 call centre, Grama/Ward Sachivalayam, Monday Collectorate hearings, mobile app.
grievanceTopics.content:
1. Call centre — Dial 1902 or 1800-425-4440
Operator registers complaint, generates YSR#, sends SMS acknowledgement.

2. Grama / Ward Sachivalayam
Visit nearest Village Secretariat or Ward Secretariat. Staff enter your complaint into the PGRS system (often 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM on working days).

3. Spandana Monday programme
Every Monday at District Collectorates, Revenue offices, and Police offices — public grievance hearings. Submit petitions physically to Collector or Joint Collector.

4. Mobile app
AP PGRS / Spandana mobile app (Android) — same registration and tracking as web portal.

5. Physical petition at Collectorate
For urgent or complex cases, attend Spandana Monday with copies of all documents.

grievanceTopics.title: Track status, timelines & escalation levels
grievanceTopics.summary: Track with YSR# on portal; 7–15 days simple issues; escalation up to CMO and beyond.
grievanceTopics.content:
How to track complaint status
Visit pgrs.ap.gov.in or meekosam.ap.gov.in → Track Grievance Status.
Enter YSR# and mobile number if required.

Status examples: Pending | Under Process | Sent to Officer | Action Taken | Closed

Typical resolution time (varies by department & verification):
• Simple issues: 7–15 days
• Revenue / land matters: 30–90 days
• Police / legal matters: varies

Escalation levels (if no action):
• Level 1 — Mandal Officer
• Level 2 — District Officer
• Level 3 — District Collector
• Level 4 — Department Head (Secretary level)
• Level 5 — CMO Monitoring Cell
• Level 6 — CPGRAMS / Court / Lokayukta (outside Spandana)

Always keep YSR#, SMS messages, and uploaded documents for appeals.

whatsappServices.title: Mana Mitra — WhatsApp Governance (official number)
whatsappServices.summary: Save 9552300009, send "Hi", choose English or Telugu, then pick a service from the menu.
whatsappServices.content:
Andhra Pradesh launched WhatsApp Governance under the name Mana Mitra (మనా మిత్ర) — government services through WhatsApp chat.

Official WhatsApp number: +91 9552300009

How to start:
1. Save 9552300009 in your phone contacts as "Mana Mitra" or "AP Government WhatsApp"
2. Open WhatsApp and send the message: Hi
3. Select language: English or Telugu
4. Choose "Select Service" or pick a department from the menu
5. Follow the chat prompts (Aadhaar, mobile, or application details where required)
6. Receive receipts, status updates, and certificates on WhatsApp / SMS

Mana Mitra connects dozens of departments. Services expanded over time (161+ in first phase; 700+ services reported in later updates). Always use only the official government number above.

whatsappServices.title: Revenue & certificate services on WhatsApp
whatsappServices.summary: Income, caste, land records, marriage certificate, ROR, and related revenue services.
whatsappServices.content:
Through Mana Mitra you can apply for or track many Revenue department services, including:

• Income Certificate / Re-issuance of Income Certificate
• Integrated Certificate / Family Member Certificate
• OBC Certificate, EWS Certificate, No Earning Member Certificate
• Agriculture Income Certificate
• ROR-1B, Computerized Adangal, Title Deed cum Passbook
• Marriage Certificate
• Encumbrance certificate (EC) related services
• Water tax and property-related queries

You select Revenue Services from the department list, choose the certificate type, enter Aadhaar and other details as prompted, and pay online if fees apply. Approved documents are delivered digitally.

whatsappServices.title: Municipal, energy, transport & civic services
whatsappServices.summary: Property tax, electricity bills, APSRTC tickets, and municipal complaints.
whatsappServices.content:
Mana Mitra also covers everyday civic needs:

• CDMA / Municipal: Know your dues, pay property tax, check application status
• Energy (DISCOM): View bills, pay bills, register and track electricity complaints
• APSRTC: Bus ticket booking and cancellation
• Police: Lost document / articles certificate and related citizen services
• PGRS on WhatsApp: Lodge grievance, check grievance status, give feedback
• CM Relief Fund: Donation / application using mobile, Aadhaar, or CMRF ID
• Civil Supplies: Deepam (LPG) status and ration-related queries

These services reduce visits to offices — useful for students and families in both urban and rural areas.

whatsappServices.title: Temple darshan, health & welfare on WhatsApp
whatsappServices.summary: Tirumala and other temple bookings, NTR Vaidya Seva, education hall tickets.
whatsappServices.content:
Other popular Mana Mitra service categories:

• Temple / Endowments: Darshan and accommodation booking for Tirumala (Srivari), Srisailam, Simhachalam, Vijayawada Kanaka Durga, Annavaram, Dwaraka Tirumala, Srikalahasti, and more
• Health: NTR Vaidya Seva card status and updates
• Education: Hall ticket download and related student services
• Agriculture & horticulture: Scheme information and farmer-related services
• Welfare schemes: Various department menus for subsidies and eligibility checks

Response time: Simple queries may be answered within 24 hours; field verification (e.g. land EC, road repair) can take 30–45 days. You receive ticket / reference numbers in chat for tracking.

whatsappServices.title: Tips for using WhatsApp governance safely
whatsappServices.summary: Use only the official number; never share OTPs or passwords with unknown chats.
whatsappServices.content:
• Use only +91 9552300009 — verify the green business badge where shown
• Do not share OTP, PIN, or bank passwords in any chat claiming to be government
• Keep Aadhaar and mobile number ready; some services require Aadhaar authentication
• Save chat receipts and reference numbers for follow-up
• For issues not available on WhatsApp, use PGRS helpline 1902 or visit your Ward Secretariat
• GSWS staff conduct awareness campaigns (e.g. Friday outreach) — ask at your local Sachivalayam for help registering on Mana Mitra

WhatsApp governance is designed for digital Andhra Pradesh — learn it as a modern civic skill alongside traditional grievance channels.
